name: Terbium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Terbium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Terbium oxide layer removal

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for optimal Terbium absorption

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning

fluenceThreshold.description: Fluence threshold for Terbium oxide removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete oxide removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for complete coverage

power.description: Typical range for Nd:YAG laser cleaning of reflective metals; starting at 100W to balance ablation and heat input. Terbium's low thermal conductivity (~11 W/mK) requires conservative power to avoid melting (mp 1356°C). Source: Extrapolated from metal cleaning studies (e.g., Kumar et al., Opt. Lasers Eng. 2018); manufacturer specs like IPG Photonics YLR series limit max to prevent substrate damage.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal on metals; higher for light contaminants (e.g., oxides), lower for heavy. Recommended 1500 mm/s for Terbium to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ) due to poor thermal dissipation. Source: Industry standards from Laser Clean Corp. manuals; case studies on rare earth alloys (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for industrial cleaning to achieve plasma-mediated ablation with minimal HAZ; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but costlier. 20 ns recommended for Terbium to limit thermal diffusion. Source: Laser physics research on metal ablation (e.g., Ancona et al., Appl. Surf. Sci. 2010); avoids fs due to Terbium's reflectivity at 1064 nm.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat accumulation in low-conductivity materials like Terbium. 30 kHz balances efficiency and safety. Trade-off: >50 kHz may cause subsurface heating. Source: Academic literature on Nd:YAG cleaning (e.g., Lee et al., J. Phys. D 2015); adapted for rare earth metals.

energy_density.description: Fluence below Terbium's ablation threshold (~4 J/cm² estimated for 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal without substrate damage. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for light industrial cleaning. Terbium's high reflectivity (~70% at IR) requires higher fluence than dielectrics. Source: Ablation threshold studies on similar metals (e.g., Rubenchik et al., Appl. Opt. 2017); conservative due to limited Terbium-specific data.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for precise cleaning; smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for Terbium surface preparation to ensure uniform fluence without over-focusing heat. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems; optimized for metal reflectivity in industrial applications.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete Terbium cleaning without excessive exposure. Multi-pass allows lower fluence per pass to protect soft substrate. Source: Industry practice from case studies on alloy cleaning (e.g., Proc. SPIE 2019); Terbium's malleability favors fewer passes.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for Terbium to avoid gaps or over-ablation, balancing speed and efficacy. Higher (70-80%) for heavy contaminants. Source: Process optimization studies (e.g., Katayama et al., J. Laser Micro/Nanoeng. 2012); standard for scanned laser cleaning of metals.
